query: pharmacology of pimavanserin vs typical antipsychotics

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: While the mechanism of action of pimavanserin differs from other atypical antipsychotics, namely the resulting lack of dopamine D2 receptor blockade, there is a remaining concern for the risk of death in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis since pimavanserin could potentially have a similar effect. Therefore, like other antipsychotics, pimavanserin has a black box warning of increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis. Pimavanserin is safe to use in patients with mild to moderate renal impairment, and dosage adjustment is not required. Pimavanserin is not recommended for use in patients with severe renal impairment or any degree of hepatic impairment. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Behavioral and Psychological Symptoms in Dementia  -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacologic Interventions for Agitation and Aggression
retrieved_docs.content: Starting maximum doses of antipsychotics for BPSD are as follows: Aripiprazole 2 mg daily and 15 mg daily, respectively; Olanzapine 2.5 mg daily and 10 mg daily; Quetiapine 12.5 mg twice daily and 100 mg twice daily; and Risperidone 0.25 mg twice daily and 1 mg twice daily. Doses can be increased in small increments every 2 weeks after insufficient improvement, based on prospective ratings from caregivers. Due to their potential to worsen motor symptoms, clinicians should avoid using antipsychotics other than Quetiapine, Pimavanserin, and Clozapine in Lewy body dementia and dementia associated with Parkinson disease. Clozapine requires special monitoring and reporting when prescribed. In the United States, Pimavanserin is approved by the Food and Drug Association for the treatment of psychosis related to Parkinson disease. However, it carries the same black box warning as other antipsychotics. [20] The starting and target dose of pimvanserine is the same (34 mg). Like other antipsychotics, it prolongs the QT interval and carries a black box warning about the increased risk of death in geriatric patients with dementia.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Antipsychotic Agents -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Pimavanserin carries a risk of QT interval prolongation and, therefore, should not be administered to individuals with a history of cardiac dysrhythmias. Adverse reactions observed with pimavanserin include rash, urticaria, and angioedema. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Pimavanserin is an antagonist/inverse agonist at the 5HT2A receptor and has a 40-fold weaker antagonism/inverse agonism at the 5HT2C receptor. [13] Unlike other atypical antipsychotics, pimavanserin lacks dopaminergic activity, namely at the D2 receptor. Pimavanserin is the first drug without dopamine D2 blocking activity that displays antipsychotic actions. Thus, fulfilling a particular niche by treating the psychotic symptoms without worsening the motor symptoms of parkinsonism, an adverse effect commonly observed with other antipsychotics. [2] Additionally, pimavanserin does not cause sedation, seen in quetiapine or clozapine, which are currently used off-label to treat PDP. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindicated drug interactions include concomitant use with pimozide or thioridazine. As already discussed, caution and dosing adjustments may be necessary with potent CYP450 inducers and inhibitors.

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological and Non-pharmacological Management Strategies:
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics. This category includes haloperidol and atypical antipsychotics. Haloperidol is a dopamine (D2) receptor antagonist. It is given at the dosage of 2-10 mg (IV every six h); it is useful, especially in the hyperactive form. Atypical antipsychotics used for this purpose are olanzapine (IM 5-10 mg; max: 30 mg/d), risperidone (0.5-8 mg), quetiapine (orally 50 mg; max 400 mg/d), and ziprasidone. A special issue concerns antipsychotic-related toxicity. For instance, cardiotoxicity such as QT interval (QT) prolongation, torsade de pointes, and hypotension are reported at high doses of haloperidol, whereas a dosage of 2 mg can be safely administered. [24] Of note, caution should be taken when antipsychotics are used with other QT-prolonging medications. Other commonly used classes of drugs that are associated with QT prolongation include, but are not limited to, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, antiemetics, and antidepressants, as well as methadone, lithium, octreotide, and tacrolimus, among others. A baseline EKG and further assessment may be warranted. Antipsychotics can also induce extrapyramidal symptoms (EPSs) expressed as parkinsonism featuring dystonic reactions or akathisia. [25] Rarely, haloperidol can cause neuroleptic malignant syndrome [26] , whereas insomnia and agitation can often be observed. In the case of atypical antipsychotics, EPSs can manifest at high doses. Again, olanzapine and quetiapine may induce excessive sedation, whereas ziprasidone is more associated with QT prolongation.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Antipsychotic Agents -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical antipsychotics with D2 antagonism and partial agonism combined with 5-HT2A antagonism are more effective for treating mania, and these include aripiprazole, quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone, and asenapine. Antipsychotics also have histamine, muscarinic (cholinergic), and α-adrenergic antagonism. Almost all atypical antipsychotics bind to α-adrenergic receptors, but the most potent are clozapine, risperidone, iloperidone, and clozapine. Quetiapine, clozapine, and olanzapine have high anticholinergic properties, whereas other atypical antipsychotics very weakly bind to muscarinic cholinergic receptors. [21] [22] [23] Samidorphan, an opioid receptor antagonist, has been co-formulated with olanzapine into a single-dose oral tablet to alleviate the risk of weight gain while maintaining olanzapine's therapeutic efficacy. [24] The mechanism by which pimavanserin is effective in delusion and hallucinations associated with Parkinson's disease psychosis remains uncertain; its efficacy may result from its dual action as an inverse agonist and antagonist at serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, alongside a lesser impact on serotonin 5-HT2C receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to Parkinson disease, psychotic symptoms can also manifest in different subtypes of dementia. At present, there are no FDA-approved agents indicated for dementia-related psychosis (DRP). Pimavanserin is under evaluation for its potential use in DRP. [4] In a phase II clinical trial of patients with Alzheimer disease psychosis, patients treated with pimavanserin versus placebo showed significant improvement in psychosis at the 6-week primary endpoint (p=0·045). The significance did not progress to the week 12 follow-up (p=0·561). [5] Notably, there was no observable negative impact on cognition in either group. [5] Further evaluation of a severe subgroup of patients with Alzheimer disease psychosis demonstrated more significant improvement of symptoms. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pimavanserin is an atypical antipsychotic that received FDA approval in the U.S. in 2016 for hallucinations and delusions that accompany Parkinson disease psychosis (PDP). [1] It is the first and only FDA-approved medication for PDP. It had initially received Breakthrough Therapy status from the FDA in 2014. In the phase III clinical trial that paved the way for FDA approval, patients receiving pimavanserin demonstrated significant improvement in the frequency and/or severity of hallucinations and/or delusions without worsening motor function compared to patients receiving a placebo. [2] A separate study using data from the same trial found a more robust response to pimavanserin in PDP patients with impaired cognitive functioning. [3] Besides its use in PDP, pimavanserin has no other FDA or non-FDA-approved indications. However, there are various ongoing studies on the potential uses of pimavanserin.

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for pimavanserin. Review the typical adverse effects of pimavanserin. Summarize the risks associated with initiating pimavanserin, summarizing key patient counseling points. Outline the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance patient care when initiating and monitoring pimavanserin to improve patient outcomes for patients receiving treatment for Parkinson disease psychosis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Pimavanserin may cause QTc prolongation. Patients should receive instructions to report any new onset palpitations, syncope, or near-syncope. Likewise, clinical illnesses such as gastroenteritis or the initiation of diuretic therapy, which could lead to hypokalemia, should also be reported as prompt identification and correction of electrolyte abnormalities minimizes the risk of arrhythmias. Pimanversin is not associated with the constellation of weight gain, hyperlipidemia, and hyperglycemia, called metabolic syndrome seen in other atypical antipsychotics. However, it is still beneficial for the patient to eat a healthy diet, engage in an exercise regimen, and abstain from or stop smoking while taking the medication. Given that pimavanserin undergoes hepatic metabolization via the same cytochrome P450 pathways as various other medications, providers should be aware of drugs that may inhibit or induce the metabolism of pimavanserin and adjust dosing accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In a study examining the discontinuation rate of patients with PDP or dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) given either pimavanserin or quetiapine, the discontinuation rate of patients treated with pimavanserin was lower earlier on and higher later on in comparison to quetiapine. [7] Outside of the realm of DRP, pimavanserin has also shown promise in treating positive as well as challenging to treat negative symptoms of schizophrenia. A recent study reported success with pimavanserin in patients with schizophrenia and schizoaffective disorder with refractory psychosis. [8] The manufacturer of the drug reported that as of March 2022, they were conducting a phase 3 clinical trial to evaluate pimavanserin for major depressive disorder and schizophrenia, as well as phase 2 trials for agitation and phase 1 trials examining its effectiveness in psychiatric disorders. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Antipsychotic Agents -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Risperidone: Carbamazepine and other inducers necessitate an increased dosage. Concurrent administration of risperidone with CYP2D6 inhibitors like fluoxetine and paroxetine requires a dosage reduction of risperidone. Pimavanserin: Pimavanserin may require dose adjustment when used concurrently with potent CYP3A inhibitors. This drug's efficacy might be diminished when administered alongside strong CYP3A inducers.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Antipsychotic Agents -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Pimavanserin is exclusively indicated for addressing hallucinations and delusions linked to Parkinson's disease psychosis. Pimavanserin is not approved for addressing psychosis in patients with dementia unless it is associated with Parkinson's disease. [35] The Society of Critical Care Medicine (SCCM) recommends against the routine utilization of haloperidol or atypical antipsychotics to prevent or reduce the duration of delirium in critically ill pediatric patients, as per a conditional low recommendation. However, in cases of refractory delirium, the SCCM suggests considering haloperidol or atypical antipsychotics for managing severe delirium manifestations, with caution regarding potential adverse drug effects, according to a conditional moderate recommendation. Furthermore, the SCCM recommends performing a baseline electrocardiogram followed by routine electrolyte and QTc interval monitoring for patients receiving haloperidol or atypical antipsychotics. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pimavanserin is a medication used to treat and manage the hallucinations and delusions associated with Parkinson disease psychosis. It is in the atypical antipsychotic class of drugs. This activity reviews the indication, action, and contraindications for pimavanserin as a valuable agent in managing Parkinson disease psychosis. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, dosing, monitoring, relevant interactions, and other key factors pertinent for healthcare team members in the care of patients with Parkinson disease psychosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Pimavanserin -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications to taking pimavanserin include hypersensitivity to pimavanserin or any of its constituents. Other contraindications or cautions include: Uncorrected electrolyte abnormalities QT prolongation or a family history of QT prolongation, including congenital long QT syndrome A history of torsades des pointes Ventricular arrhythmias Recent history of myocardial infarct Bradycardia Congestive heart failure Caution is also recommended in the elderly, patients with end-stage renal disease (ESDRD) or a CrCl below 30, and patients with psychosis related to dementia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: Prochlorperazine edisylate and chlorpromazine hydrochloride are both classified as phenothiazine compounds, indicating that they belong to the same chemical class of antipsychotic and antiemetic agents. This shared classification reflects common pharmacologic properties inherent to phenothiazine drugs, such as dopamine receptor antagonism, which underlies their therapeutic use in managing psychosis, nausea, and related conditions. Recognizing that both medications are phenothiazines helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential side effect profiles, and considerations for patient care when selecting or monitoring treatment within this drug class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1552
retrieved_docs.content: Acepromazin is classified as an antipsychotic medication. The drug class antipsychotics also includes pimozid, indicating that both acepromazin and pimozid belong to the same therapeutic category of agents used to treat psychotic disorders. Recognizing these drugs as members of the antipsychotic class helps clinicians understand their shared pharmacologic properties and consider them when selecting appropriate treatment options for patients with psychosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Bromodiphenhydramine and pimavanserin are pharmacologic agents that may be employed in the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. Bromodiphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity, has potential utility for alleviating motor symptoms associated with the neurodegenerative disorder. Pimavanserin, a selective serotonin‑5‑HT₂A inverse agonist, is also recognized for its possible therapeutic role in Parkinson’s disease, particularly for managing psychotic features and may provide broader symptomatic benefit. Recognizing both drugs as possible treatment options expands therapeutic considerations for clinicians managing patients with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1353
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified as a dopamine antagonist, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that block dopamine receptors. As a member of this pharmacologic class, promazine exerts its effects by inhibiting dopaminergic neurotransmission, a mechanism commonly utilized in the treatment of psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia and in managing certain types of nausea. Understanding that promazine functions as a dopamine antagonist helps clinicians anticipate its therapeutic actions as well as potential side effects related to dopamine blockade, such as extrapyramidal symptoms, sedation, and anticholinergic effects. This classification informs appropriate patient care decisions, including drug selection, dosing, and monitoring for adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is identified as a hormone antagonist, while SB 206553 is listed among serotonin antagonists. A broad group of compounds is classified as anxiolytic agents, including binospirone mesylate, bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, deramciclane, diazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, fluvoxamine, medazepam, meprobamate, midazolam, ritanserin, triazolam and ondansetron. Although ondansetron is primarily used as an antiemetic for chemotherapy‑induced nausea, it appears in the data as an anxiolytic, indicating a possible classification inconsistency. Anxiolytics are reported to be associated with methotrimeprazine, a compound also classified as an antipsychotic. Diazepam, medazepam and meprobamate are further categorized as centrally acting muscle relaxants that reduce skeletal muscle tone via central nervous system mechanisms; diazepam is thus both an anxiolytic and a muscle relaxant. Antipsychotic agents mentioned include acepromazine, pomaglumetad methionil and methotrimeprazine, which are used to manage psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia and are distinct from the serotonin‑antagonist group. Serotonin antagonists identified are ondansetron, SB 206553, pindolol, binospirone mesylate, cyanopindolol, deramciclane and ritanserin; they block serotonergic receptors and have varied indications from nausea control to experimental modulation of mood and perception. Fluvoxamine, while related to the serotonin system, functions primarily as a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor rather than a direct antagonist. Together, these relationships illustrate overlapping pharmacologic categories—anxiolytics, centrally acting muscle relaxants, antipsychotics, serotonin antagonists and hormone antagonists—and underscore the importance of careful interpretation when a drug is listed under multiple therapeutic classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2233
retrieved_docs.content: Ondansetron is a medication that belongs to the class of serotonin (5‑HT3) receptor antagonists, which block serotonin receptors to prevent nausea and vomiting. Antipsychotics constitute a separate pharmacologic category used to treat psychotic disorders, and acepromazine is an example of a drug that is classified as an antipsychotic (a phenothiazine tranquilizer). Although both ondansetron and acepromazine are drugs, they are members of distinct therapeutic classes with different mechanisms of action; ondansetron is not an antipsychotic, while acepromazine exemplifies the antipsychotic class. Recognizing these differences aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments and avoiding confusion between medication categories.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1094
retrieved_docs.content: this association is not consistent with current pharmacological knowledge. Mirtazapine’s primary actions involve antagonism of central α2‑adrenergic receptors and selective serotonin receptors, not muscarinic antagonism. Likewise, the classification of benzazepines as an inverse “isa” of mirtazapine does not reflect established drug taxonomy. Therefore, ipratropiumbromid and other anticholinergic inhalants remain distinct from mirtazapine and benzazepine compounds in both mechanism and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1364
retrieved_docs.content: Perfenazin and chlorpromazine‑hydrochloride are both members of the phenothiazine class of compounds. Perfenazin is a derivative (a child) of phenothiazine, indicating it belongs to the phenothiazine subclass, while chlorpromazine‑hydrochloride is directly classified as a phenothiazine. This hierarchical relationship highlights that both agents share the core phenothiazine chemical structure and typically exhibit similar pharmacologic actions, such as dopamine receptor antagonism used in the management of psychiatric disorders. Recognizing that perfenazin and chlorpromazine‑hydrochloride are situated within the phenothiazine family helps clinicians understand their mechanisms, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 466
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics comprise a pharmacologic group that includes several phenothiazine‑derived agents such as methotrimeprazine, perfenazin, acepromazine, promazine, and chlorpromazine‑hydrochloride. Phenothiazine is the core chemical class from which many of these drugs are derived, and it functions both as a subclass of antipsychotics and as a subclass of antiemetics. Consequently, phenothiazine derivatives such as promazine and chlorpromazine‑hydrochloride are used to treat psychotic disorders through dopamine‑receptor antagonism, while the same structural family also provides antiemetic effects for conditions like vomiting alone. Dopamine antagonism is a key mechanism shared by these agents, linking the concept of “dopamine antagonist” with the antipsychotic class. Because of potential drug‑interaction concerns, antipsychotic medications are contraindicated when used together with the antibiotic lefamulin acetate. This integrated view highlights the overlapping therapeutic roles of phenothiazine‑based compounds in managing both psychiatric symptoms and nausea, as well as the importance of recognizing contraindications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified both as an antiemetic and as a dopamine antagonist. As an antiemetic, promazine is used to prevent or treat nausea and vomiting, while its role as a dopamine antagonist means it blocks dopamine receptors, a mechanism that contributes to its anti‑nausea effects and can also be relevant in managing conditions such as psychosis or movement disorders. The overlap between these categories highlights that many dopamine‑blocking agents, including promazine, possess antiemetic properties, which is important for clinicians when selecting appropriate therapy for patients who require control of nausea or benefit from dopamine receptor inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1562
retrieved_docs.content: Pimozide is contraindicated for use with sertraline, indicating that these two medications should not be co‑administered because their combination can lead to harmful drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1407
retrieved_docs.content: Mirtazapin exerts its pharmacological effects through a cholinergic muscarinic antagonism mechanism, classifying it among agents that block muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. In contrast, mirtazapin is not a member of the benzazepiny chemical class, indicating that its structural and pharmacological profile differs from benzazepiny compounds. This distinction clarifies that while mirtazapin’s therapeutic action is mediated by muscarinic receptor blockade, it does not share the typical characteristics of benzazepiny agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are linked to خلل الحركة المتأخر, a late‑onset movement disorder (commonly known as tardive dyskinesia) that can develop after prolonged use of these medications; recognizing this relationship is essential for clinicians to monitor patients on antipsychotic therapy for abnormal involuntary movements and to adjust treatment strategies to reduce the risk of this adverse effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2307
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, chlormethiazol, kabergolin, and pimavanserin are agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease. Parkinson’s disease is clinically associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor). Recognizing both the potential therapeutic options and the motor and non‑motor clinical associations underscores the importance of comprehensive management for patients with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 51
retrieved_docs.content: these hierarchical classifications, pharmacodynamic properties, and contraindications is essential for safe prescribing, prevention of harmful drug interactions, and effective management of psychiatric and withdrawal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1475
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrimeprazine, acepromazine, and pomaglumetad methionil are all members of the antipsychotic drug class. As typical antipsychotics, these agents are used to manage psychotic disorders by modulating neurotransmitter pathways, particularly dopamine signaling. A notable clinical consideration for antipsychotics, including methotrimeprazine, acepromazine, and pomaglumetad methionil, is their association with late‑onset movement abnormalities, such as tardive dyskinesia (referred to in Arabic as “خلل الحركة المتأخر”). Awareness of this potential adverse effect is essential for clinicians when prescribing and monitoring patients receiving these antipsychotic medications.
